page.content: In the ongoing pursuit of understanding the differential impact of biomarkers in neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder (NMOSD), the data presented in Table 1 provides a comprehensive overview of various modeling approaches employed to assess the significance of specific predictors among patient cohorts. The table delineates the outcomes of conventional regression analyses, focusing on the variable of serum analysis as a predictor.

lines.text: In the ongoing pursuit of understanding the differential impact of biomarkers in neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder
(NMOSD), the data presented in Table 1 provides a comprehensive overview of various modeling approaches employed to
assess the significance of specific predictors among patient cohorts. The table delineates the outcomes of conventional
regression analyses, focusing on the variable of serum analysis as a predictor.

page.html:
MethodPredictor of the modelBasis typeType of model
M1XNoneConventional regression
M2WB-splinesFunctional regression (Eq. 8)
M3WFourierFunctional regression (Eq. 8)
M4X*B-splinesAlternative 1 for Functional regression (Eq. 9)
M5X*FourierAlternative 1 for Functional regression (Eq. 9)
M6X**B-splinesAlternative 2 for Functional regression (Eq. 10)
M7X**FourierAlternative 2 for Functional regression (Eq. 10)

page.content: Notably, the patient groups are categorized based on the presence of aquaporin-4 (AQP4) antibodies, with AQP4(−) patients comprising 16 individuals and AQP4(+) patients encompassing 53 individuals. The findings underscore the distinct biochemical profiles that may contribute to differing clinical manifestations and disease progression in these subgroups. Moreover, the column detailing P values serves to highlight the statistical significance of the relationships observed, offering crucial insights into the predictive validity of the models employed. This analysis not only enhances our understanding of NMOSD pathophysiology but also underscores the importance of tailored therapeutic strategies that consider the underlying immunological differences between AQP4(−) and AQP4(+) patients. Such insights are imperative for advancing personalized medicine approaches in the management of this complex neurological disorder.
